systems.name: Directional Picking
systems.summary: Bruno's central system: three directional stroke rules (down to higher string, up to lower string, alternate on same string) applied across all textures (scales, arpeggios, skips, bebop). Justified by economy of motion. The system enumerates the stroke-type vocabulary as members and codifies both the core rules and the sanctioned exceptions (rest start, triplet drill, big-band emphasis, rhythmic break, hard skip+accent override).

members.name: Up-down stroke
members.summary: Inverted-accent stroke; upstroke carries the downbeat.

members.name: Mixed strokes
members.summary: Alternates accents between down and up.

members.name: In-between stroke
members.summary: Hardest stroke; pick moves between two strings (adjacent or skipped).

members.name: Consecutive down-stroke
members.summary: Multiple notes on adjacent strings picked with successive down-strokes.

members.name: Consecutive up-stroke
members.summary: Multiple notes on adjacent strings picked with successive up-strokes.

members.name: Same-string sixteenth reversal
members.summary: Direction reversal occurring on a single string with repeated notes.

traversal_rules.name: Down-stroke when crossing to a higher string
traversal_rules.summary: When the next note is on a higher string, use a down-stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: When going to a higher string, use a down-stroke

traversal_rules.name: Up-stroke when crossing to a lower string
traversal_rules.summary: When the next note is on a lower string, use an up-stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: When going to a lower string, use an up-stroke

traversal_rules.name: Alternate strokes on the same string
traversal_rules.summary: When two consecutive notes lie on the same string, alternate down and up.

references.quote_excerpt: When playing on one string, use alternate strokes

traversal_rules.name: Economy of motion governs traversal
traversal_rules.summary: The pick lands where it next needs to be; minimize travel between notes on different strings.

references.quote_excerpt: one concept or rule that causes the pick to travel the least amount of distance

traversal_rules.name: String-skip directional rule
traversal_rules.summary: Directional rule applies identically when skipping non-adjacent strings: down to higher, up to lower.

references.quote_excerpt: When skipping strings the same rule applies

traversal_rules.name: Directional rule applies across all six major-scale fingerings
traversal_rules.summary: Down-on-higher / up-on-lower governs every string crossing in the 6V2/5V2/6V4/5V4/6H2/5H2 fingerings.

references.quote_excerpt: six basic fingerings for the major scales

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio traversal is the same directional rule
traversal_rules.summary: Sweep-style arpeggios are not a separate technique; they are the higher/lower rule on arpeggio notes.

references.quote_excerpt: Higher string = down stroke Lower string = up stroke
picking for single lines is different than picking for arpeggios

traversal_rules.name: Fingerboard position dictates picking pattern
traversal_rules.summary: Relocating an identical phrase to a different position changes which notes land on which strings, thus changing the stroke sequence.

references.quote_excerpt: same phrase in the 7th position. This changes the picking considerably

modification_rules.name: Triplet exception: three down then three up
modification_rules.summary: For repeating triplet arpeggio patterns across three adjacent strings (drill only), suspend alternation and use three consecutive downs then three consecutive ups.

references.quote_excerpt: when playing triplets with a repeating pattern... it is better to break the rule
three down-strokes followed by three up-strokes

modification_rules.name: Triplet exception confined to drill, not real phrases
modification_rules.summary: In actual melodic lines, the triplet exception reverts to strict alternation.

references.quote_excerpt: if I had to play a triplet phrase in the context of a line... I would adhere to the rule

modification_rules.name: Phrase after rest may start with either stroke
modification_rules.summary: When a rest separates phrases, the next phrase can begin with either a down- or an up-stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: when there is a rest between phrases, it is possible to break the rule

modification_rules.name: Consecutive down-strokes for big-band accent
modification_rules.summary: To emulate big-band horn-section accents, use consecutive down-strokes to emphasize specific notes.

references.quote_excerpt: consecutive down strokes to emphsis certain notes... big band type phrase

modification_rules.name: Slurs as substitute for consecutive downs
modification_rules.summary: The same big-band phrase may be articulated with slurs instead of consecutive down-strokes.

references.topic: Slurs as alternative to consecutive downs
references.quote_excerpt: same type phrase with slurs

modification_rules.name: Rhythmic break licenses two down-strokes
modification_rules.summary: When a rhythmic break separates notes, two consecutive down-strokes may be used instead of strict alternation.

references.topic: Rhythmic breaks justify two down strokes
references.quote_excerpt: rhythmic break between notes, you may want to use to down strokes

modification_rules.name: Hard skip+accent override to alternate picking
modification_rules.summary: For the combination of in-between stroke, string skip, and up-stroke accent, Bruno prefers strict alternate down-up picking over his own directional rule.

references.quote_excerpt: easier to play them with alternating down - up strokes

modification_rules.name: Prefer slurred triplets over picked triplets in bebop
modification_rules.summary: In bebop phrases, slurred triplets are preferred over picked triplets because they sound more horn-like.

references.quote_excerpt: I prefer the 2nd example; it sounds more horn-like

modification_rules.name: Slurring substitutes pick strokes with hammer/pull
modification_rules.summary: Articulation is produced by picking one note and sounding two via hammer-on or pull-off, replacing a pick stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: Pick one note and play two notes

modification_rules.name: Slurs require notes on the same string
modification_rules.summary: Hammer-on and pull-off articulation requires the two notes to lie on a shared string.

references.quote_excerpt: moving the slur over any two notes that are on the same string

modification_rules.name: Long-short ending articulation
modification_rules.summary: Phrase endings articulated with a long-short rhythmic gesture on the last two notes.

references.quote_excerpt: play these last two notes, long, short

preferences.name: Practice every exercise starting with both strokes
preferences.summary: Each exercise must be drilled starting from both a down-stroke and an up-stroke; tonal difference should be slight.

references.quote_excerpt: practice all the exercises starting with both strokes
Try starting any phrase with both strokes, down and up
START THIS EXERCISE WITH BOTH STROKES, DOWN AND UP

preferences.name: Precision over speed
preferences.summary: Rhythmic evenness of 8th notes is more important than tempo at the consecutive-strokes stage.

references.quote_excerpt: precision of the 8th notes is more important than speed

preferences.name: Articulation over speed in arpeggios
preferences.summary: Keeping arpeggio notes from ringing into each other is more important than playing fast.

references.quote_excerpt: Articulatioin is more important han speed

preferences.name: Fingering, not picking aggression, enables speed
preferences.summary: Arrange arpeggios on adjacent strings so the directional rule yields fluent consecutive strokes; speed follows from fingering choice.

references.quote_excerpt: the fingering makes the speed possible
fingerings that place notes on adjacent strings

preferences.name: Music drives picking, not the fretboard
preferences.summary: The picking mechanism serves musical intent; the fingerboard must not dictate the line.

references.quote_excerpt: NOT let the fingerboard make the music

preferences.name: Notes must not ring into each other
preferences.summary: Consecutive-stroke and in-between-stroke drills require muting; sustained ringing is disallowed.

references.quote_excerpt: Do not let notes ring!
Do not let notes ring!

preferences.name: Practice tempo MM 60-120
preferences.summary: All exercises are practiced starting at MM 60-120 before increasing speed.

references.quote_excerpt: practice these exercise at MM 60 to 120

preferences.name: Sequential study order; no skipping
preferences.summary: Exercises must be studied A-to-Z; the student should not skip around the book.

references.quote_excerpt: not a good idea to skip around in this book

preferences.name: Repetition over analysis
preferences.summary: Drill strokes until they become automatic; do not overanalyze right-hand movements mid-stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: key is repetition. Do not overanalyze your right hand movements

systems.name: Right-Hand Mechanics
systems.summary: The physical preconditions for the directional-picking system: motion from the elbow with a still wrist, and a strict no-anchor right hand whose fingers do not touch the strings, pick guard, or guitar body.

members.name: Wrist
members.summary: Held straight but not stiff; does not tilt or drive motion.

members.name: Right-hand fingers
members.summary: Must float free of all contact surfaces.

members.name: Palm
members.summary: May rest lightly on the strings.

traversal_rules.name: Motion comes from the elbow
traversal_rules.summary: Picking motion is generated at the elbow; the wrist remains still and untilted.

references.quote_excerpt: The movement should come from the elbow
the movement is from the elbow

modification_rules.name: Right-hand fingers must not touch the guitar
modification_rules.summary: Fingers must not contact strings, bridge, or any other part of the guitar.

references.quote_excerpt: Do not touch the strings, bridge or any other part of the guitar
Do not touch the guitar with the fingers of the right hand

modification_rules.name: No pick-guard contact
modification_rules.summary: Right-hand fingers must not touch the pick guard or strings, reinforcing free-floating posture.

references.quote_excerpt: Do not touch the pick guard or strings

modification_rules.name: Relaxed right hand, wrist, and elbow
modification_rules.summary: The right hand, wrist, and elbow must remain relaxed; never tense or tight.

references.quote_excerpt: always be relaxed and never tense or tight

systems.name: Six Essential Major-Scale Fingerings
systems.summary: Bruno's V/H taxonomy of six core major-scale shapes used as the substrate for applying the directional picking rule in Chapter 4. V denotes two-notes-per-string verticals; H denotes position-shift horizontals; the numeral indexes the root string.

traversal_rules.name: String-crossing notation marks where the directional rule applies
traversal_rules.summary: Asterisks above scale-fingering exercises mark higher- vs lower-string moves, making the directional rule visible at every transition.

references.quote_excerpt: ** = moving to HIGHER STRING
* = moving to LOWER STRING

modification_rules.name: Anchored to Six Essential Fingerings system
modification_rules.summary: The six shapes are keyed to Bruno's companion book Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist, which is the framework these picking exercises apply to.

references.quote_excerpt: see my book Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist
